Agent Zero 3-Tier Memory System Architecture:

TIERS:
1. Short-Term Memory: Current conversation context, automatically managed and cleared between sessions
2. Long-Term Memory: Persistent storage for facts, solutions, and code fragments using vector embeddings
3. Knowledge Base: Custom document library for semantic search

STORAGE STRUCTURE:
- Location: ~/agent-zero/memory/ with subdirectories
- Three organizational areas within Memory Dashboard: MAIN (general memories), FRAGMENTS (information snippets), SOLUTIONS (problem solutions)
- Storage subdirectories: facts/, solutions/, fragments/
- Uses metadata with IDs, timestamps for filtering/searching

STORAGE BACKENDS:
- Configurable via MEMORY_VECTOR_DB: chroma, faiss, qdrant (default: faiss)
- FAISS uses IndexFlatIP (inner product similarity search) for exact matching
- Persists through Flask API backend with MemoryStore interface
- Files stored in JSON format in subdirectories

PROMOTION/DEMOTION:
- Memories with importance score > 0.7 promoted from short-term to long-term before removal
- Automatic extraction of facts, problem-solution pairs, reusable code when enabled
- Pruning: memories older than 90 days with minimal access removed
- Consolidation: duplicate/redundant information merged

EMBEDDING MODEL:
- Default: nomic-embed-text (1024 dimensions, 8192 token context)
- Also supports: all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (384 dims), mxbai-embed-large (1024 dims)
- Can be configured via MEMORY_EMBEDDING_MODEL env var
- Changing model requires re-indexing all stored memories
- Supports Ollama, LMStudio, OpenAI providers

DEDUPLICATION:
- Vector similarity threshold-based: MEMORY_SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD=0.7 (default)
- System identifies and merges overlapping memories through consolidation process
- Prevents duplicate storage through similarity-based filtering on retrieval
- No explicit hash-based fingerprint deduplication mentioned; relies on vector similarity

SCALE/CONFIGURATION:
- MEMORY_MAX_RESULTS=10 (max memories returned per query)
- MEMORY_SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD=0.7 (min relevance score 0-1)
- CONTEXT_COMPRESSION_THRESHOLD=0.8 (manages context window limits)
- MEMORY_SAVE_INTERVAL=300 (saves every 5 minutes)
- Supports multi-tenant isolation per subdirectory (singleton FAISS per subdir)
- No published benchmark numbers found; system designed for "near-infinite short-term memory"
- Full CRUD operations via Memory Dashboard web UI

RESEARCH: Additional AI Agent Hive-Mind Architecture Options (beyond original 6) - March 2026

OPTION 7: FEDERATED LEARNING / WEIGHT-SHARING BRAIN
- Knowledge is implicit in model weights, not in explicit knowledge records
- Agents share gradient updates or LoRA adapters, not raw data
- Key techs: Flower framework (flower.ai), FedEx-LoRA, SDFLoRA, FedALT
- Server aggregates via FedAvg or smarter weighted aggregation
- LoRA variant: each agent fine-tunes low-rank adapters, server merges them (together.ai does multi-LoRA serverless)
- Newest: Federated Reinforcement Learning for agents learning from environment
- Pros: privacy-preserving, no raw data leaves agents, knowledge is baked into weights
- Cons: high communication cost, catastrophic forgetting, heterogeneity problems, slow convergence
- Best for: privacy-critical deployments, medical/legal AI, where raw data cannot be shared

OPTION 8: LATENT SPACE / NEURAL COMMUNICATION BRAIN
- Agents communicate by passing raw hidden states (not tokenized text)
- Interlat paper (arxiv 2511.09149): agents share last-layer hidden states directly
- LatentMAS (arxiv 2511.20639): training-free, 14.6% accuracy gains, 70.8-83.7% fewer tokens, 4x faster
- Vision Wormhole (arxiv 2602.15382): cross-modal latent sharing between heterogeneous agents
- Key: shared embedding space = the knowledge database; forward pass = retrieval
- MaaS (arxiv 2506.22815): Memory as a Service, decouples memory from agents, routable memory containers
- MemOS (arxiv 2505.22101, 2507.03724): memory OS for LLMs, MemCube abstraction, 159% improvement on LoCoMo benchmark
- Pros: higher information density than token communication, faster inference
- Cons: agents must share same or compatible architecture, requires fine-tuning alignment

OPTION 9: EVENT SOURCING / CQRS KNOWLEDGE BACKBONE
- All knowledge = immutable events appended to a log
- Apache Kafka/Pulsar/Redpanda as the event backbone
- Agents write: produce events (knowledge contributions) to topics
- Agents read: consume from topics, maintain materialized views (local read models)
- New agents: replay event log to reconstruct full history
- CQRS: separate read path (materialized views) from write path (event log)
- EventSourcingDB released OpenCQRS 1.0 in late 2025
- Kafka 4.1.1 now KRaft-only (no ZooKeeper)
- Confluent blog: Kafka + Apache Flink = agentic AI backbone; A2A + MCP + Kafka enterprise pattern
- Pros: complete audit trail, replay for new agents, exactly-once semantics, temporal queries
- Cons: event schema evolution hard, storage grows unbounded, eventual consistency
- Best for: compliance-heavy domains, time-series knowledge, audit requirements

OPTION 10: DHT-PRIMARY / CONTENT-ADDRESSED KNOWLEDGE STORE
- Pure DHT as the database, not just for discovery
- AGNTCY ADS (arxiv 2509.18787): Kademlia-based DHT for agent capability routing
- Content-addressed: hash(knowledge) = key, knowledge = value
- AGNTCY uses OCI artifacts + Sigstore provenance + DHT routing
- Holochain: agent-centric, each agent has local source chain + publishes to shared DHT only when needed
- Holochain: ~50ms publish, ~30ms retrieval, no global ledger
- Learned DHT (arxiv 2508.14239): ML models embedded in DHT for optimized range queries
- BitTorrent-style: many seeders of popular knowledge, auto-replicated
- Pros: no central server, scales horizontally, content integrity by hash
- Cons: Kademlia lookup is O(log n) hops, cold knowledge retrieval slow, DHT churn
- Best for: fully decentralized deployments, censorship-resistant knowledge

OPTION 11: STREAMING MESSAGE QUEUE AS KNOWLEDGE BACKBONE
- Kafka/Pulsar/NATS/Redpanda as primary knowledge bus
- Agents = producers/consumers of knowledge streams
- Different from event sourcing: here streaming IS the store (log retention), not just transport
- Apache Flink for stream processing + ML inference on knowledge streams
- NATS JetStream: lightweight, edge-compatible, 1M+ msgs/sec
- Kafka A2A integration: Google A2A + MCP over Kafka for enterprise agentic AI
- StreamNative blog: "data streaming to agentic AI" as core pattern
- Topic partitioning = knowledge domain sharding
- Pros: proven at LinkedIn/Slack/Netflix scale, real-time knowledge propagation, replay
- Cons: not a vector store (needs separate search index), operationally complex
- Best for: high-throughput event-driven agent networks, real-time knowledge sharing

OPTION 12: EDGE/SERVERLESS ACTOR MODEL (CDN-NATIVE BRAIN)
- Cloudflare Workers + Durable Objects: each agent runs as stateful actor at edge
- Vectorize (Cloudflare's vector DB) for semantic search at edge
- Workers AI: inference at edge, 300+ PoPs globally
- Actor model: each knowledge chunk = a Durable Object (has own SQLite, WebSocket, scheduler)
- CodeCRDT (arxiv 2510.18893): CRDT-based agent coordination, strong eventual consistency
- Cisco Unified Edge (Nov 2025): enterprise platform for distributed agentic AI
- Pros: zero cold start, <50ms latency globally, scales to 10M+ instances, no infrastructure to manage
- Cons: Cloudflare vendor lock-in, 128MB memory limit per DO, not designed for large ML models
- Best for: globally distributed agents with low-latency requirements

OPTION 13: POST-QUANTUM CRYPTOGRAPHIC KNOWLEDGE LAYER (cross-cutting)
- Not a standalone architecture but a provenance/trust layer
- ML-DSA (CRYSTALS-Dilithium, FIPS 204): signs every knowledge contribution
- ML-KEM (Kyber): encrypts knowledge in transit and at rest
- AWS KMS, ACM, Secrets Manager now PQC-enabled (2025)
- Agent Name Service (ANS): proposed IETF standard mapping agent IDs to PQ keys + capabilities
- Use case: every knowledge write is signed with agent's PQ key → tamper-evident provenance
- Threat: Sybil attacks, registry poisoning, MITM on knowledge retrieval
- Best layered on top of options 1-12 rather than used alone

OPTION 14: STIGMERGY / SWARM ENVIRONMENT MODIFICATION
- Agents share knowledge indirectly by modifying a shared environment (not by sending messages)
- Digital stigmergy: agent writes to shared vector DB as "pheromone trail"
- Subsequent agents read and amplify (positive feedback) or decay (negative feedback = forgetting)
- Pheromone decay = knowledge recency/relevance weighting
- Saab/Swedish Armed Forces: stigmergy for 100-drone swarm (2025)
- Antetic AI: stigmergy applied to LLM agent coordination
- Similar to P2P gossip but without explicit message passing
- Best for: emergent collective behavior, no central coordinator needed

OPTION 15: MESH RADIO / OFFLINE-FIRST P2P
- For air-gapped or infrastructure-free deployments
- LoRa + Meshtastic: off-grid mesh radio, tested at 1km urban / 8-15km line-of-sight
- Offline LLM on Meshtastic: fully offline, no internet required (HackerNews 2024)
- EFF lobbying for expanded LoRa spectrum rights (July 2025)
- Knowledge sync: gossip protocol over radio mesh, CRDT for conflict resolution
- Very low bandwidth (LoRa ~250bps to 5.5kbps) limits knowledge to small facts/embeddings
- Best for: disaster response, military AI agents, remote field deployments

OPTION 16: DNA / BIOLOGICAL ARCHIVAL STORAGE
- Ultra-long-term knowledge archival (millennia timescale)
- 1 gram DNA = ~455 exabytes storage
- Atlas Data Storage: Atlas Eon 100, first scalable DNA storage service (2025)
- Fraunhofer Institute: synthetic DNA mass storage demonstrations (May 2025)
- Market: $150M in 2025 → $44B by 2034
- Read latency: hours to days (PCR + sequencing pipeline)
- Write latency: hours (DNA synthesis)
- Not suitable for real-time agent reads, only archival/compliance
- Best for: regulatory knowledge archival, civilization-scale AI memory backup

OPTION 17: AI-TO-AI PROTOCOL STANDARDS (AITP / A2A / ANP / AGNTCY)
- Emerging standard layer for agent interoperability including knowledge sharing
- AITP (NEAR, Feb 2025): threads-based, blockchain-backed trust, payments-capable
- A2A (Google, Jun 2025): 50+ partners, Linux Foundation project
- AGNTCY (Jul 2025, Linux Foundation): DHT discovery + OCI artifacts + gRPC
- AAIF (Dec 2025): Anthropic MCP + OpenAI AGENTS.md + Block goose under Linux Foundation
- These are protocol standards, not storage systems - define HOW agents share, not WHERE
- ANP (Agent Network Protocol): community-driven, JSON-LD based
- Best as: interoperability layer on top of any storage backend (options 1-16)

ACADEMIC RESEARCH (2025):
- MemOS (MemTensor): Memory OS treating memory as system resource. MemCube as basic unit (content + provenance + versioning). Five states: Generated→Activated→Merged→Archived→Expired. Time-to-live and frequency-based decay. Published May 2025.
- KARMA: 9-agent LLM pipeline for knowledge graph enrichment (entity discovery, relation extraction, schema alignment, conflict resolution)
- AnchorRAG: predictor agent + parallel retriever agents + supervisor agent for multi-hop KG traversal

- OFKGE: federated KG embedding, clients train local, server aggregates via Adaptive Semantic Alignment

RESEARCH: Centralized AI Agent "Hive Mind" Architecture - Two Options (March 2026)

OPTION 1 - Managed Vector DB + Federation:
- Pinecone: up to 100,000 namespaces/index, RBAC with 6 API key roles (3 control plane, 3 data plane), serverless pricing $0.33/GB storage + $8.25/1M reads + $2.00/1M writes. Standard $50/month minimum, Enterprise $500/month. Supports millions of namespaces for agentic workloads. Optimized for sparse namespaces with blob storage + on-demand caching.
- Weaviate: RBAC GA in v1.29, OIDC/OIDC-groups, schema-based tenant isolation, $25/1M vector dimensions/month (serverless), $45/month minimum. Multi-tenant with dedicated shards per tenant. Built-in vectorization.
- Qdrant: SSO (Okta/Google/Azure/Ping/SAML), per-cluster/per-collection API keys, conditional updates for concurrent writes, tiered multitenancy (1.16), self-hosted cost ~$102/month for starter, 1B vectors @ 100QPS ~$2200/month managed.
- Milvus: Kafka/Pulsar/Woodpecker pub/sub backbone, WAL for durability, K8s-native, horizontal scale, separates storage (S3/MinIO) from compute.
- Conflict resolution: last-writer-wins is unsafe; need orchestrator-mediated serialization, role/confidence-aware schemes, voting mechanisms.
- Provenance: metadata fields (agent_id, timestamp, confidence, evidence_source) on every vector entry.

OPTION 2 - Federated Knowledge Graph (Neo4j Aura):
- Neo4j Aura Agent (GA 2025): $100M investment, Agentic Brain with persistent memory + Context Graphs, Infinigraph for 100TB+ horizontal scale.
- Composite Databases (Fabric evolution): cross-graph Cypher queries over distributed graphs, local + remote constituents.
- Graphiti (Zep): temporal knowledge graph, bi-temporal data model (event time + ingestion time), conflict resolution via semantic/keyword/graph search, invalidates but preserves outdated facts, LongMemEval +18.5% accuracy, 90% latency reduction vs baseline.
- MemOS: three-layer architecture (API / scheduling / storage), MemCube abstraction, supports multi-agent memory sharing via MemStore/MemVault, 72% lower token usage.
- GraphRAG: entity/relationship extraction → communities → multi-hop reasoning; slower for dynamic data (Microsoft), better for stable domain knowledge.
- Schema evolution: SchemaAgent + CriticAgent pattern for dynamic ontology evolution; LPG model is schema-optional.
- Hybrid: vector for semantic similarity + graph for structured traversal; Mem0 uses both simultaneously.

COST AT SCALE (1000 agents):
- 1B vectors @ 100 QPS: Pinecone ~$3,500/month, Weaviate Cloud ~$2,200/month, self-hosted Weaviate ~$800/month.
- Neo4j Aura: starts $65/month, hourly metering, scales to enterprise pricing.
- Self-hosted Qdrant on K8s: cheapest managed operational cost but requires DevOps.

QUALITY CONTROL:
- LLM-as-Judge: confidence scoring + uncertainty quantification before writes; ~80% human agreement on generic tasks, 60-68% on expert domains.
- Multi-agent voting/ensemble before commit.
- Graphiti: semantic dedup + temporal invalidation (non-lossy).
- Microsoft MARA: traceability fields mandatory (session_id, agent_id, timestamp) on all entries.

RESEARCH: HuggingFace / GitHub / GitLab as AI Agent Hive Mind Knowledge Layer (March 2026)

HUGGING FACE HUB:
- Rate limits (5-min windows): Free=1000 API calls, PRO=2500, Team=3000, Enterprise=6000, Enterprise-Plus+IP-ranges=100,000
- Resolver (file download) limits: Free=5000, Team=20,000, Enterprise=50,000
- Storage: Free public=best-effort (few GB practical), Private=100 GB; PRO=10TB public + 1TB private; Team=12TB+1TB/seat; Enterprise=200TB+1TB/seat
- Private storage: $18/TB/month base, public add-on $12/TB/month
- Push rate limit: undocumented but enforced; commit-level rate limits exist (granular, not published)
- VECTOR SEARCH: Three options: (1) Parquet+DuckDB+VSS extension — no-index works <100K rows (~30s query), HNSW index brings to sub-second but requires local copy; (2) FAISS index stored alongside dataset (add_faiss_index, save/load); (3) Lance format (native Feb 2026) — bundles embeddings+HNSW index on Hub, can query without downloading, 10-20x faster than streaming API
- DuckDB can query via hf:// protocol directly: SELECT with array_cosine_distance over parquet files
- HF Inference Endpoints for embedding: TEI $0.06/CPU-hr, GPU from $0.5/hr; free-tier credits for experimentation
- HF Spaces: free CPU basic (2vCPU/16GB), can host FastAPI/Gradio as query API; ZeroGPU free H200 access
- HF MCP Server: exposes model/dataset/space search + semantic docs search + run jobs. Gradio spaces expose as MCP tools.
- OpenEnv Hub (Meta+HF, Nov 2025): standardized agentic environments
- smolagents: lightweight HF agent framework, model-agnostic

GITHUB:
- API rate limits: authenticated=5000 req/hr, GitHub App=15000 req/hr, GITHUB_TOKEN in Actions=1000 req/hr per repo
- Secondary limits: max 100 concurrent requests, 1-second delay recommended between write requests
- Repo size: 10 GB recommended max; file size 100MB hard limit, 1MB recommended; push limit 2GB/push
- Push rate: 6 pushes per minute per repo (recommended)
- Git LFS: 2GB/file (free), 4GB (Team), 5GB (Enterprise); batch API 100 objects/request
- GitHub Models embedding: free tier 15 req/min, 150 req/day, 64K tokens/req; Copilot Enterprise: 20 req/min, 450/day
- Agentic Memory (Jan 2026, public preview): stores memories as {subject, fact, citations, reason} objects; citations=file paths+line numbers; citation verification at retrieval time; memories expire after 28 days; shared across coding agent + code review + CLI; repo-scoped (write access to create, read access to retrieve)
- GitHub Flat Data: GitHub Action that fetches external data and commits to repo on schedule
- GitRows: REST API over GitHub/GitLab repos for JSON/CSV data (free for public repos)
- Dolt: git-for-data SQL database — 1.8x slower than MySQL, full branch/merge/clone semantics
- Prior art: GitHub Blog explicitly building agentic memory on Git repos (citations as provenance)

GITLAB:
- API rate limits (GitLab.com): authenticated general = 7200 req/hr (2/sec avg); configurable on self-managed
- Repository Files API: 3 req/30 sec for requests >20 MB; standard small commits unconstrained at that specific limit
- Commits API: 300MB max request size; same large-file rate limit
- Free tier: 400 CI/CD minutes/month, 5 users max, 10 GiB storage
- Self-hosted: unlimited storage (set limit=0), unlimited CI minutes with own runners, configurable rate limits
- GitLab Knowledge Graph (beta, v18.4): scans codebase for files/classes/functions/relationships, MCP server via `gkg` CLI, available free through Ultimate; uses semantic search + RAG for agent accuracy; foundation for Knowledge Graph Service 2026
- GitLab Duo Agent Platform (public beta): custom agents, CLI, official MCP server, deep research agent with semantic search
- CI/CD for quality pipeline: 400 free minutes/month on .com; own runners = unlimited

KEY WRITE THROUGHPUT ESTIMATES (realistic):
- HF: ~100-500 knowledge items/hour before hitting undocumented commit rate limits; batch via DatasetBuilder recommended
- GitHub: 6 pushes/min = ~360/hr theoretical; in practice 50-200 items/hr accounting for merge logic
- GitLab.com: similar to GitHub; self-hosted = effectively unlimited with own runners

VECTOR SEARCH SUMMARY:
- HF: Native (Lance format Feb 2026) — best option; DuckDB+HNSW index — excellent for <1M rows
- GitHub: No native vector search; must use GitHub Search API (keyword only) or external index
- GitLab: Knowledge Graph has semantic search but limited to code entities; no general vector store API

SCALABILITY:
- 10K items: All three work fine
- 100K items: HF with Lance/DuckDB HNSW — works; GitHub — keyword search only, slow; GitLab — needs KG or external
- 1M items: HF with Lance on Hub — viable; GitHub — repo bloat, git performance degrades; GitLab KG — unclear
- 10M+ items: None of the three are designed for this; need hybrid with local vector DB

IDENTITY/AGENT WHITELISTING:
- DID (Decentralized Identifiers) + Verifiable Credentials standard for agent identity
- did:kite pattern: did:kite:user.eth/agent/role-v1 encodes authority chains
- Multilayer revocation: P2P propagation + cryptographic certificate invalidation + economic slashing

ORACLE/VALIDATION PATTERNS:

HIVE FRAMEWORK RESEARCH SUMMARY (February 28, 2026)

## What it does:
- Open-source outcome-driven AI agent framework for building autonomous, self-improving agents without hardcoding workflows
- Goal-first design: describe objectives in natural language → system auto-generates agent graphs with dynamic connection code
- Production-ready framework for long-running autonomous agents handling real business processes

## Architecture:
1. **Coding Agent**: Generates agent graphs and connection logic from natural language objectives
2. **Worker Bees**: SDK-wrapped nodes executing tasks with shared memory, monitoring, tool access
3. **Judge Node**: Evaluates outcomes against defined criteria and principles
4. **Queen Bee**: Central orchestrator managing system-wide decisions and escalations
5. **Infrastructure Layer**: Event bus, credential store, conversation memory, shared state management
6. **GraphExecutor**: Orchestrates sequential execution of nodes, detects fan-out/fan-in, supports concurrent branch execution via asyncio.gather()

## How it extends Claude Code:
- Integrates with Claude Desktop via .claude/ directory structure
- Skills defined in .claude/skills/ with domain-specific capabilities (hive-concepts, hive-create, hive-credentials, hive-patterns, hive-test, hive-debugger)
- Supports `/hive` command in Claude Code for agent building
- MCP integration for tool discovery - can register STDIO and HTTP transport MCP servers
- AgentBuilder tools server exposes agent-building capabilities as MCP endpoints

## Memory/Learning capabilities:
- Self-adaptation loop: captures failures → analyzes outcomes → evolves agent graph → redeploys
- Conversation memory and shared state management via EventBus
- Node history tracked in Runtime execution records
- Outcome-based evaluation stores results for pattern learning

## Gate/Enforcement system:
- Human-in-the-loop intervention nodes with configurable timeouts
- Cost limits and budget enforcement with automatic model degradation
- Worker health monitoring via secondary Health Judge graph
- Structured escalation tickets on degradation signals
- Credentials management system: OAuth2, encrypted file storage, Vault integration, Aden sync support

## Token efficiency:
- DEFAULT_MAX_TOKENS constant defined in GraphExecutor
- Model-agnostic via LiteLLM supporting 100+ providers
- Automatic model degradation policies on budget overflow
- Configurable timeout controls for human-in-the-loop to prevent idle token burn

## Speed/Parallelism:
- Concurrent branch execution: asyncio.gather() for fan-out scenarios
- Convergence detection algorithm for fan-in synchronization
- Configurable branch failure handling: fail_all, continue_others, wait_all
- Node checkpointing for resumable execution

## Reliability/Error handling:
- Exponential backoff retry logic (1s, 2s, 4s...) up to configurable max_retries
- Distinguishes "clean" vs "degraded" successes
- Retry tracking per node
- Self-healing loop: failures trigger automatic re-architecture

## Test coverage:
- Built-in testing framework in core/framework/testing/
- LLM-based judge for test evaluation
- Approval workflow tooling for test validation
- Test categorization and debug tools
- test_case.py, test_result.py, test_storage.py, llm_judge.py, approval_cli.py

## Lines of code / Complexity:
- Repository: 7.8 MB, 8,467 stars, 4,811 forks
- Language breakdown: Python 94.6%, TypeScript 4.9%, Shell 1.1%, PowerShell 1.0%
- Core modules: graph, llm, mcp, credentials, runtime, runner, observability, monitoring, testing, tui, tools, storage
- LLM providers: Anthropic, LiteLLM, Mock
- 60+ active contributors, substantial weekly activity
- Pre-built agents: credential_tester, hive_coder (code generation agent)

RESEARCH: Hybrid Hot/Warm/Cold Architecture for AI Agent Knowledge Networks (Option 6)

THREE-LAYER ARCHITECTURE:
- Hot layer (L1): centralized vector DB (Pinecone, Qdrant, Weaviate, Redis vectors). In-memory, <10ms query latency. Active/recent knowledge. AWS S3 Vectors launched 2025 as managed vector storage.
- Warm layer (L2): P2P gossip (GossipSub/OrbitDB) for inter-cluster propagation. Session-persistent, ~10ms-seconds access. Knowledge survives conversation turns but may expire after inactivity.
- Cold layer (L3): blockchain/IPFS for permanent, auditable archive. Content-addressed (CIDs), immutable, provenance-tracked. Minutes to hours access time but eternal storage.
- Reputation layer: lightweight DAG or blockchain (e.g., Substrate parachain, or even a simple Merkle accumulator) for contribution tracking without full blockchain overhead.

MIGRATION FLOW:
Hot → Warm: triggered by access pattern (cooling off) or time-based (e.g., 24h TTL in hot)
Warm → Cold: triggered by consensus (multiple agents verified the knowledge) or time (e.g., 7 days in warm)
Cold → Hot: explicit retrieval request, cached into hot layer for session duration

HYBRID RAG PATTERN (2025 standard):
- Vector index (FAISS/HNSW) for semantic similarity
- Inverted index (BM25) for keyword match
- Knowledge graph (Neo4j/FalkorDB) for structured relationships
- Combined at query time via RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion) or cross-encoder reranking

LAYER TRADEOFFS:
- Hot: fast, cheap queries, expensive storage, centralized failure risk, not permanent
- Warm: medium latency, free propagation, eventual consistency, no permanence
- Cold: permanent, auditable, expensive write operations, slow reads, trustless

AGENT INTERACTION PATTERNS:
- New knowledge → write to hot layer immediately, gossip to warm async
- Query → check hot first, fall back to warm DHT lookup, then cold IPFS retrieval
- Validation → warm layer agents vote on quality, threshold triggers cold archival
- Reputation updates → batched to blockchain/DAG periodically (not per-knowledge-item)

RESEARCH: Telegram as AI Agent Hive Mind Knowledge Layer — Key Technical Findings (March 2026)

CRITICAL ARCHITECTURAL CONSTRAINT:
Bot-to-bot message invisibility: Telegram Bot API deliberately prevents bots from seeing messages sent by OTHER bots in a group. This is intentional (prevents feedback loops). No workaround within Bot API. This breaks the naive "all agent bots post to group, organizer bot reads all" architecture.

SOLUTION: The organizer must use MTProto (via Pyrogram/Telethon) running as a userbot/service account, not as a standard bot. This lets it see all messages including bot messages. Agent bots can use standard Bot API to post.

RATE LIMITS (confirmed):
- Per group: 20 messages per minute per bot
- Per chat: 1 message per second max
- Global bot: 30 messages per second across all chats
- Paid broadcast (100k+ MAU, 100k+ Stars): up to 1000 msg/s at 0.1 Stars/msg
- deleteMessage: max 100 IDs per call, channel deletes ~20/sec
- getUpdates: returns earliest 100 unconfirmed updates

FILE LIMITS (Bot API):
- Upload: 50 MB max (2 GB with local Bot API server)
- Download by bot: 20 MB max (2 GB with local server)
- With MTProto user account: 2 GB (4 GB Premium)

MESSAGE LIMITS:
- Text message: 4,096 characters
- Media caption: 1,024 chars (4,096 Premium)
- Format: JSON in text up to 4KB, or as .json document attachment (50MB)

GROUP STRUCTURE:
- Max bots per group: 20 (critical limit for multi-agent swarms)
- Max members: 200,000 (supergroup), unlimited (gigagroup/broadcast group)
- Admins: up to 50
- Forum topics: up to 1,000,000 per group
- Pinned topics: 5

HISTORY ACCESS:
- Bot API (getUpdates): only returns recent unprocessed updates, no true history search
- MTProto (Pyrogram search_messages): full-text search with filters, works on bots AND users
- Pyrogram get_chat_history(): retrieves all messages with offset pagination, up to 200/call
- tg-archive (open source): Telethon-based, sync to SQLite, incremental, hits rate limits on large groups

CHANNEL vs SUPERGROUP for this use case:
- Supergroup with Forum mode: BEST — bots can post, topics map to domains, up to 200k members
- Channel: organizer-only posts, agents post to linked discussion group — clean separation of verified vs raw knowledge
- Gigagroup: no member writes, admin-only — too restrictive for agents contributing

E2E ENCRYPTION: NOT available for groups or bots. All group/channel messages are server-encrypted only (Telegram can read). Secret chats = 1:1 only, no bot support.

SEARCH CAPABILITIES:
- Bot API: NO built-in search method
- MTProto search_messages(): query string, filter by type, filter by sender, paginated — YES this works
- Inline mode: agents can query organizer bot via @organizer_bot query from any chat — powerful UX

INLINE QUERY PATTERN (key for retrieval):
- Agent types "@organizer_bot search: knowledge about X"
- Organizer responds with up to 50 results as InlineQueryResults
- Results are ephemeral, not stored, no rate limit specified but standard flood protection applies
- Cache time configurable (default 300s)

MCP SERVERS EXIST:
- sparfenyuk/mcp-telegram: MTProto-based, full account access
- IQAIcom/mcp-telegram: Bot API, send/forward/pin/get members
- fast-mcp-telegram: PyPI package

EXISTING PRIOR ART:
- tg-archive (knadh): Telethon → SQLite sync, incremental, for archiving not knowledge management
- telegram-knowledgebase-bot (Zifius): basic /def /wtf glossary bot, abandoned, SQLAlchemy backend
- Telegram Multi-Agent AI Bot (Furkan-Gulsen): PTB + LangChain + MongoDB Atlas, Telegram as UI layer not storage
- NO prior art found for using Telegram group chat natively as the vector knowledge store

ORGANIZER BOT DESIGN:
Must run as MTProto service account (not bot token) to see all bot messages. Maintains local LanceDB/SQLite. Responds to inline queries. Handles dedup, quality scoring, deletion.

PROS vs other options:
+ Free permanent storage (Telegram pays for it)
+ Zero infra cost for storage
+ Built-in access control (group membership)
+ Audit trail (all posts timestamped, attributed)
+ Human-readable (humans can browse the knowledge base)
+ Forum topics = domain partitioning
+ Reactions = quality voting
+ Pinned = canonical verified knowledge
+ 4GB file attachments (with Premium) for embedding dumps
+ Global CDN for file delivery
+ Works as notification channel to humans simultaneously

CONS / DEALBREAKERS:
- Bot-to-bot invisibility requires MTProto workaround (complexity)
- 20-bot-per-group limit caps agent count
- 20 msg/min per bot is slow for high-throughput ingestion
- No E2E encryption — Telegram servers can read everything
- 4,096 char text limit means large knowledge items need file attachments
- Telegram could ban/rate-limit/block abusive bot accounts
- Message history not guaranteed (bots have limited cloud storage for updates)
- Search is text-only (no native vector/semantic search — must build externally)
- Telegram ToS may prohibit automated bot-to-bot communication at scale

Master Brain / Hive Mind research — 6 architecture options for shared AI agent knowledge network:

Option 1: Managed Vector DB + Federation (Pinecone/Weaviate/Qdrant/Milvus) — agents connect via API, namespace-based multi-tenancy, staging→verified promotion pipeline, archivist agent for quality. Cost: $600-2800/mo at 1000 agents. Best for: semantic recall, fast queries, simple ops.

Option 2: Federated Knowledge Graph (Neo4j Aura / Graphiti/Zep) — agents contribute nodes+edges, multi-hop traversal, contradiction as first-class edges, bi-temporal tracking. Cost: $300-1000/mo + LLM ingestion. Best for: causal reasoning, explainability, structured domains.

Option 3: Public Blockchain (Solana/NEAR/Bittensor) — hashes on-chain, data on IPFS/Arweave, stake-weighted quality voting, token incentives. Challenge window 24-48h for validated knowledge. Oracle costs dominate ($1K-50K/day). Best for: open global ecosystem, permissionless contribution.

Option 4: Private Blockchain (Avalanche Subnet/Hyperledger Fabric) — whitelisted agents only, X.509+DID identity, sub-second finality, no gas fees, selective bridge to public chain. Cost: $250-4000/mo infra. Best for: trusted consortium, compliance, fast finality.

Option 5: P2P Gossip + CRDTs (libp2p/GossipSub/OrbitDB) — no servers, no blockchain, agents form mesh, web-of-trust reputation, eventual consistency. Zero infra cost. Best for: censorship resistance, zero cost, offline-first.

Option 6: Hybrid "Best of All Worlds" — Hot layer (vector DB, <10ms), Warm layer (P2P gossip, seconds), Cold layer (IPFS+blockchain, permanent archive), Reputation DAG. Knowledge flows hot→warm→cold over time. Best for: production systems at scale.

Key cross-cutting concerns: embedding model alignment (mandate single model), JSON-LD schema standardization, W3C PROV provenance chains, TTL-based knowledge decay, privacy-preserving sharing (federated learning, compute-to-data).

Existing projects: Bittensor (TAO, $3.3B mcap, 128+ subnets), ASI Alliance (Fetch+Ocean+SingularityNET, $9.2B), Graphiti/Zep (temporal KG), Ceramic Network, MemOS (academic), NEAR Shade Agents.

RESEARCH: Cross-Cutting Design Patterns for Agent Knowledge Networks

1. KNOWLEDGE SCHEMA STANDARDIZATION:
- JSON-LD: maps JSON properties to web ontology (RDF-compatible). Standard for linked data interoperability.
- W3C RDF/OWL: semantic web standards enabling machine-readable ontologies
- MCP as "USB-C for AI": standardizes how agents access data sources. June 2025 spec update added formal auth roles + token protection.
- GraphRAG (2025 standard): combines vector embeddings + graph structure for richer retrieval
- DBpedia 2025-06: supports multimodal AI agents + real-time KG reasoning

2. EMBEDDING MODEL ALIGNMENT:
- Critical challenge: agents using different embedding models/versions have misaligned vector spaces
- Solution: standardize embedding model across entire agent ecosystem (e.g., all use nomic-embed-text-v2-moe or text-embedding-3-large)
- Always normalize (L2 normalization for cosine similarity)
- Cross-model translation: vec2vec neural network approach (arxiv 2306.12689) for bridging different embedding spaces
- FedGALA: unsupervised contrastive learning to align GNNs + frozen PLMs in federated settings

3. PROVENANCE CHAINS:
- Blockchain+IPFS: Merkle DAG for content-addressed storage, blockchain for immutable transaction log
- MemOS ProvAPI: each memory tagged with provenance ID containing: event triggers, contextual state, model ID, external links, timestamps
- Knowledge lineage: parent→child chain linking derived knowledge to source
- Immutable audit trail: nonrepudiation by design in blockchain-based systems

4. FORGETTING / DECAY:
- MemOS five states: Generated→Activated→Merged→Archived→Expired
- Policies: time-to-live (TTL), frequency-based decay (low access = archive), task-scoped expiry
- Multi-agent coordinated forgetting: dedup shared experiences, merge overlapping traces
- GossipSub score decay: peer scores decay over time (time-weighted)
- Knowledge pruning: agents maintain local freshness scores, obsolete entries garbage collected

5. PRIVACY-PRESERVING KNOWLEDGE SHARING:
- Federated Learning: train locally, share gradients/model updates (not raw data)
- Differential Privacy: add calibrated noise to shared updates (epsilon-delta guarantees)
- Secure Multi-Party Computation: compute over private data without revealing inputs
- Homomorphic Encryption: compute on encrypted data
- Ocean Protocol compute-to-data: algorithm goes to data, data never leaves owner
- FedRL (Federated Reinforcement Learning): agents share abstract policies, not experiences

HIVE FRAMEWORK - COMPREHENSIVE TECHNICAL RESEARCH (Feb 28, 2026)

## EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
Hive (github.com/adenhq/hive) is a production-grade outcome-driven agent framework for autonomous agents executing real business processes. Unlike vanilla Claude Code (general coding assistant) or simple prompt-based agents, Hive provides:
- Automatic agent graph generation from natural language goals
- Self-improving agents via failure-driven evolution
- Triangulated verification (deterministic rules + LLM + human judgment)
- Full production infrastructure (observability, credentials, cost control)
- Multi-agent coordination with shared memory management
- Repository: 8,415 stars, 4,777 forks, v0.5.1 (Feb 2026), Python 3.11+

## ARCHITECTURE

### Core Concept: Triangulated Verification
Instead of trusting a single oracle (e.g., LLM output alone), Hive uses THREE convergent signals:
1. Deterministic Rules - fast constraint checks, known failure patterns
2. Semantic Evaluation - LLM-based assessment with confidence gating
3. Human Judgment - reserved for uncertain/high-stakes decisions
This ordering prioritizes cost efficiency by checking cheapest signals first.

### Five Core Subsystems
1. **Event Loop Node** - ingests external triggers (schedulers, webhooks, Server-Sent Events)
2. **Worker Bees** - execute tasks via dynamic graph structure with shared memory
3. **Judge Node** - evaluates outcomes using triangulated signals; decisions: ACCEPT/RETRY/REPLAN/ESCALATE
4. **Queen Bee** - central orchestrator for system-wide decisions and escalations
5. **Infrastructure** - event bus, credential store, conversation memory, shared state manager

### Runtime Stack (Execution Order)
AgentRunner → AgentRuntime → ExecutionStream → GraphExecutor
- **AgentRunner**: loads agents, configures tools/LLM, public API
- **AgentRuntime**: lifecycle management, entry point routing, event bus
- **ExecutionStream**: per-entry-point queues, session persistence
- **GraphExecutor**: node traversal, tool dispatch, checkpoints; supports concurrent branches via asyncio.gather()
- **EventBus**: pub/sub for NODE_STARTED, TOOL_CALL_COMPLETED, CLIENT_OUTPUT_DELTA, CLIENT_INPUT_REQUESTED

### Graph Architecture (Directed Acyclic or Cyclic)
- **Single Node Type**: `event_loop` (changed in v0.5.1 from heterogeneous types)
- Each node: multi-turn LLM reasoning with tool access; supports reflexion pattern (attempt → evaluate → learn → retry)
- **Edges**: success/failure conditions, conditional logic, LLM-determined paths, parallel branches with reconvergence
- **Shared Memory**: scoped key-value store; access boundaries enforced (nodes declare read/write sets)
- **Human-in-the-Loop**: client_facing=True nodes pause for structured input (text, multiple choice, approvals); sessions resume across hours/days

## TOKEN & CONTEXT MANAGEMENT

### Token Configuration
- Global default: `~/.hive/configuration.json` with `max_tokens` (default 8192)
- Per-agent override in `config.py`
- Model-agnostic via LiteLLM (100+ providers: OpenAI, Anthropic, Cerebras, Groq, etc.)

### Context Efficiency
- Context NOT injected via CLAUDE.md (Torus-style) but via Goals System:
  - **Goals**: structured objects with Success Criteria (weighted + llm_judge/output_contains/custom), Constraints (hard/soft), Context (domain knowledge)
  - Context auto-injected into every LLM call
- Zero-cost logging via ContextVar (async-safe, thread-safe)
- Automatic model degradation on budget overflow
- Configurable timeouts for human-in-the-loop to prevent idle token burn

### Cost Tracking
- Per-node metrics captured: LLM calls, retry counts, timing
- Structured execution logs indexed by goal, status, node
- Budget enforcement at runtime (hard constraint violations escalate)

## MEMORY SYSTEM

### Short-Term Memory
- Session-scoped key-value shared state
- Per-node isolation levels: ISOLATED (no access) | SHARED (read/write) | SYNCHRONIZED (transactional semantics)
- Managed via SharedStateManager with explicit access boundaries

### Long-Term Memory
- Durable scratchpad via memory tools (built-in MCP tools)
- Sessions persist to disk: state files + checkpoints at node boundaries
- Resume capability: pass saved state to run() to continue from checkpoint
- Planned features: Message Model & Sessions, Proactive Compaction

### Persistence Storage
- Default: ~/.hive/agents/{name}/sessions/ (FileStorage-based)
- Per-session: state file + execution logs
- Checkpoints at node boundaries for fault tolerance

## MULTI-AGENT CAPABILITY

### Runtime Support
- **Multiple Graphs**: v0.5.1 introduced Multi-Graph Runtime (load_agent, unload_agent)
- **Concurrent Execution**: StreamRuntime dict of Run objects
- **Coordination**:
  - SharedStateManager for cross-stream state
  - OutcomeAggregator unifies goal progress
  - EventBus for async event-based coordination
  - Explicit rejection of "tools-as-shared-memory" for production

### Isolation & Synchronization
- 3 isolation levels per shared state: ISOLATED, SHARED, SYNCHRONIZED
- Fan-out/fan-in detection in GraphExecutor
- Convergence detection algorithm for fan-in synchronization
- Configurable branch failure handling: fail_all, continue_others, wait_all

## LEARNING & SELF-EVOLUTION

### Four-Stage Evolution Cycle
1. **Execute** - agents run against real inputs, producing outcomes + decisions
2. **Evaluate** - results checked against success criteria and constraints
3. **Diagnose** - structured analysis: which node failed, what data, why
4. **Regenerate** - coding agent (Hive Coder v0.5.1+) modifies graph (prompts, nodes, edges, tools, constraints)

### What Changes in Evolution
- Prompts (refining LLM instructions on misunderstandings)
- Graph structure (adding validation nodes, fallback paths, loops)
- Edge conditions (adjusting routing logic)
- Tools (swapping or adding capabilities)
- Constraints (tightening based on achievability)

### Key Limitation
Evolution increases **reliability**, NOT general reasoning ability. Biological analogy: "A species doesn't 'learn' to survive winter—individuals with thicker fur survive, and that trait gets selected." Agents become robust at ENCOUNTERED problems, not entirely novel situations (require human intervention).

### Coding Agent (Hive Coder)
- Introduced in v0.5.1 as meta-agent
- Invoked via `hive code` or Ctrl+E escalation
- Generates other agents from natural language goals
- Enables continuous agent architecture evolution

## QUALITY & RELIABILITY MECHANISMS

### Gate/Constraint System (NOT pre-tool-use hooks like Torus)
Instead of mechanical tool-blocking gates, Hive uses:
- **Outcome-driven constraints**: hard (non-negotiable, escalate immediately) + soft (flexible, can modify)
- **Human-in-the-loop nodes**: client_facing=True pauses execution for human approval
- **Judge nodes**: ACCEPT/RETRY/REPLAN/ESCALATE decisions based on evaluation
- **Structured output validation**: Pydantic auto-retry on invalid JSON
- **Credential encryption**: secure storage at ~/.hive/credentials with optional Fernet encryption

### Reflexion Pattern
- Worker-Judge reflexion loop: execute → evaluate → adjust → retry
- Rich failure context enables targeted replanning, not blind retries
- Iteration limit per node prevents infinite loops

### Error Recovery
- Exponential backoff retry logic: 1s, 2s, 4s, ... up to configurable max_retries
- Fallback paths in edge routing
- Distinguishes "clean" vs "degraded" successes
- Graceful degradation: surfaces uncertainty explicitly (cannot handle → uncertain → can handle → best fit)

### Decision Logging (Critical)
- All LLM decisions logged with reasoning
- Essential fuel for failure diagnosis and targeted evolution
- Enables root-cause analysis vs. guesswork

## SPEED & PERFORMANCE

### Parallelism
- Concurrent branch execution via asyncio.gather()
- Convergence detection for fan-in synchronization
- Event-loop based (async-first) architecture

### Latency Overhead
- No explicit metrics published
- Logging overhead: zero-friction via ContextVar (thread-safe, async-safe)
- MCP tool invocation: standard RPC (Fast MCP ≥2.0.0 dependency)
- Graph traversal: event-driven, efficient for async workloads

### Cost/Speed Trade-offs
- Model degradation on budget overflow (e.g., GPT-4 → GPT-3.5-turbo)
- Configurable timeout controls for human-in-the-loop
- Automatic retry backoff prevents CPU/token thrashing

## OBSERVABILITY & MONITORING

### Structured Logging
- Automatic trace context propagation via ContextVar
- Correlation IDs: trace_id, execution_id, goal_id, agent_id, node_id
- Dual output modes: JSON (production) + human-readable (development)
- Environment control: LOG_FORMAT, ENV variables

### Real-Time Dashboards
- Terminal UI dashboard: `hive tui` command
- Agent picker (Ctrl+A), streaming output, PDF attachments
- WebSocket streaming for live execution monitoring
- Multi-graph commands (v0.5.1+)

### Traces Indexed By
- Goal status
- Node execution order
- Retry counts, LLM costs, timing
- Tool calls and outputs

## COMPARISON TO VANILLA CLAUDE CODE

| Aspect | Claude Code | Hive Framework |
|--------|-------------|----------------|
| **Design** | General-purpose coding assistant | Specialized agent framework |
| **Architecture** | Prompt → code suggestion | Natural language goal → auto-generated graph |
| **Self-Improvement** | None | Four-stage evolution cycle |
| **Testing** | Standard testing frameworks | Built-in LLM-based judge + approval workflow |
| **Multi-Agent** | Not designed for it | Native support via StreamRuntime |
| **Memory** | None (per-conversation) | Persistent STM + LTM + checkpoints |
| **Observability** | Basic console output | Full structured tracing + TUI dashboard |
| **Guardrails** | None | Constraints, human-in-the-loop, judge decisions |
| **Production Readiness** | Not aimed at deployment | Full lifecycle: execution → evaluation → evolution |
| **Cost Control** | No budget management | Explicit limits + model degradation |
| **Token Management** | No context optimization | Automatic injection via Goals |

Incremental Clustering code review findings (2026-03-11, Toroidal-teams branch):

BUG 1 — cluster_id hash collision + silent data loss (MEDIUM):
- fnv1a_hash() returns 8 hex chars (format(h,'016x')[:8]). The cluster_id is f"cl_{fnv1a_hash(content)[:12]}" — but the hash is only 8 chars long, so [:12] actually gives all 8 chars: "cl_XXXXXXXX" (11 chars total). NOT 12 meaningful chars as intended — the [:12] slice is a no-op beyond the 8-char output. This is benign but the comment/intent is misleading.
- INSERT OR IGNORE means: if two different contents hash to the same 8-char prefix, the second call inserts nothing but still returns the first content's cluster_id. The vector for the second content is NOT stored as a new cluster centroid. The new_id is appended to _cache with vec_norm but the DB has the ORIGINAL centroid. Cache and DB diverge after hash collision.

BUG 2 — cache not refreshed after INSERT OR IGNORE collision (LOW):
- After INSERT OR IGNORE, the code does: self._cache.append((new_id, vec_norm, 1)). If the insert was ignored (hash collision), the cache now has a duplicate entry for new_id with the new vector, while DB has the original. On the NEXT _load_cache() the duplicate disappears but until then cosine comparisons see the phantom entry.

SUSPICION — label update only on count%10==0 (DESIGN CHOICE, not a bug):
- Label is only regenerated when member count is a multiple of 10. For small clusters the label is set at creation time (from first member's content) and may be stale.

CORRECT items verified:
- _KNOWLEDGE_SCHEMA has cluster_id field (line 191)
- CLUSTER_THRESHOLD = 0.7 (line 1520)
- Running mean: new_centroid = (c_vec * count + vec_norm) / new_count, then unit-normalized (lines 1745-1748) — mathematically correct
- fail-open in remember_this() (lines 3671-3676): bare except, pass
- format_summaries() surfaces cluster_id only when present (lines 2137-2138)
- --bootstrap-clusters CLI flag (line 101), _bootstrap_clusters() (line 5018), __main__ handler (line 6035)
- _cluster_label() uses regex [a-zA-Z_]{4,}, Counter.most_common(3), "/" join, stopword set (lines 1784-1844)
- _ensure_initialized() declares global _cluster_store (line 1854), initializes fail-open (lines 1895-1901)
- _ClusterStore._cache initialized to None (line 1682), lazy-loaded on first assign() call

TEST COVERAGE gaps:
- No test for hash collision / INSERT OR IGNORE behavior
- No test for label staleness (count%10 update cadence)
- No test for bootstrap function directly
- All 7 main clustering unit tests covered: _cluster_label, first cluster, similar join, dissimilar new, centroid math, cache consistency, zero-vector fail-open
- Tests skip gracefully when memory server is running (to avoid LanceDB lock)

Dual-Layer Memory Architecture Survey (March 2026 research):

EPISODIC vs SEMANTIC DISTINCTION:
- Episodic: specific events/interactions with temporal grounding (what happened, when, where, who). Append-only, timestamped. For audit/replay, multi-hop temporal reasoning.
- Semantic: generalized facts, preferences, profiles abstracted from experience. Selective write, embedding-indexed. For efficient general-knowledge retrieval.

KEY PAPERS (2025-2026):
1. HiMem (2601.06377, Jan 2026): Episode Memory + Note Memory (facts/prefs/profiles). Topic-Aware Event-Surprise Dual-Channel Segmentation. Conflict-aware Reconsolidation. LOCOMO: 80.71% GPT-Score (vs A-MEM 51.88%, Mem0 68.74%).
2. Synapse (2601.02744, Jan 2026): Unified episodic-semantic graph. Spreading activation retrieval (BM25+embeddings, fan-effect, lateral inhibition, sigmoid). LoCoMo F1: 40.5 overall, 95% token reduction, $0.24/1k queries.
3. SimpleMem (2601.02553, Jan 2026): Semantic lossless compression. L0/L1/L2 multi-view indexing (semantic embeddings + BM25 + symbolic metadata). Recursive consolidation at τ=0.85. GPT-4.1-mini: 43.24 F1 with 531 tokens vs Mem0 34.20/973 tokens. 30x token reduction.
4. A-MEM (2502.12110, NeurIPS 2025): Zettelkasten-inspired. LLM-generated keywords/tags/links on write. Dynamic memory evolution (new memories update existing ones).
5. Mem0 (2504.19413): Two-phase Extraction+Update pipeline. 26% relative improvement over OpenAI memory on LOCOMO (66.9% vs 52.9%). 91% lower latency, 90% fewer tokens.

40% IMPROVEMENT CLAIM: No specific "40% improvement for dual-layer vs flat" paper found. Closest data: Synapse +7.2 F1 over A-MEM; HyMem multi-hop 88.16% vs full-context 46.89% (~88% improvement in multi-hop). SimpleMem 26.4% F1 improvement over Mem0. The claim may conflate multiple results or be an informal generalization.

WRITE POLICIES (practical):
- Episodic: append-only on every event, TTL 30 days, provenance tagged
- Semantic: LLM-extracted distillation, dedup by hash+content, selective on significance threshold
- Consolidation: background async process, cluster by semantic similarity (τ~0.85), synthesize into higher-level abstractions

RETRIEVAL PATTERNS:
- Dual retrieval: episodic (last N events) + semantic (top-k embedding search) in parallel
- Merge: dedup by source+hash, rerank by relevance+freshness, token budget trim
- Conflict resolution: prefer freshest episodic; surface both with resolution note if conflict

LANCEDB SPECIFIC: Used as vector storage for semantic layer. Hybrid retrieval (vector + BM25) is the dominant pattern. Multi-view indexing (dense + sparse + symbolic metadata) recommended for best results.

RESEARCH: P2P Gossip Protocol + CRDT Architecture for AI Agent Knowledge Networks (Option 5)

KEY TECHNOLOGIES:
- libp2p: modular P2P networking stack used by IPFS, Ethereum, Filecoin. Provides transport, muxing, NAT traversal, peer discovery
- GossipSub v1.1: pub/sub protocol with score function for peer ranking. Flood publishing for own messages, mesh for propagation. Score function tracks: first-message deliveries, mesh message deliveries, invalid messages, app-specific scoring. Largely immune to eclipse attacks.
- IPFS Kademlia DHT: O(log N) discovery across millions of peers. Content-addressed via CIDs (SHA-256 hashes). No central coordinator.
- OrbitDB: serverless P2P database on IPFS + libp2p pubsub. Uses Merkle-CRDTs for conflict-free merges. Types: events (append-only log), documents (JSON), keyvalue.
- GunDB: offline-first graph database, DAM (Daisy-chain Ad-hoc Mesh) transport protocol. Custom CRDT (commutative replicated data type). SEA crypto layer (ECDH key exchange, signing, optional PoW rate limiting). Browser+server peers.

CRDT TYPES FOR KNOWLEDGE:
- State-based CRDTs: full state exchanged, merged via join operation
- Operation-based CRDTs: only operations broadcast, require reliable delivery
- Merkle-CRDTs (OrbitDB): DAG-based, hash-addressed, content-deduplicated
- Strong eventual consistency: all replicas converge once updates propagate

PEER DISCOVERY (new agent joining):
1. Bootstrap nodes (hardcoded known peers with static addresses)
2. Kademlia DHT walk (O(log N) hops to find peers by XOR distance)
3. Rendezvous protocol (register presence at rendezvous points by namespace)
4. mDNS for local network discovery
5. GossipSub peer exchange (peers share known peers in PRUNE messages)

SYBIL RESISTANCE WITHOUT BLOCKCHAIN:
- GossipSub score function: penalizes invalid messages, rewards fast delivery, prunes negative-score peers
- Web of Trust: social graph vouching (BrightID, Circles UBI patterns)
- SybilPSIoT: Bayesian inference over trust paths, game-theoretic cost-benefit for peer admission
- MeritRank: decay parameters in graph algorithms for theoretical Sybil guarantees
- Economic cost: computational PoW (GunDB SEA has optional PoW for rate limiting)

SCALABILITY:
- Gossip overhead is O(k * log N) messages per broadcast where k=fanout (~6-12 peers)
- Pure gossip can have high communication overhead at scale; SpanningFL (MST+gossip hybrid) shows 30.7% comms reduction
- DHT lookup O(log N), IPFS proven to millions of nodes
- GossipSub mesh D parameter: default 6, D_low=4, D_high=12

ADVANTAGES: zero infra cost, censorship resistant, no single point of failure, no trust required in central party
DISADVANTAGES: eventual consistency (seconds to minutes for global propagation), harder quality enforcement, sybil risk, NAT traversal complexity, browser P2P limited
